what_it_does: This file defines a single structured decision record (a Python dict named DECISION) that documents the visual-fidelity trade-off for the company: how much visuals should be drawn in-house versus generated as photoreal imagery, with options, a recommended option, provenance, and legibility metadata.
observations: The entire file is one DECISION dictionary with id 'visual-fidelity'.
It lists three mutually exclusive options, and only the first carries 'recommended': True.
The decision's bar is stated as 'indistinguishable from real'.
It includes an 'explanation_source' URI pointing to code://build-prep/the-one-application/DECISION-GATHER.md#D1 with a provenance comment.
It has a 'legibility' block with name, is, and why fields intended to make the decision readable.
standouts: The recommended option is 'Draw everything; generate only hero showpieces'.
The provenance is explicitly anchored to a gather source section (D1) with a 'verbatim-verified, section-anchored' comment.
The decision frames the core tension as drawn (fast, on-brand, plateaus below photoreal) versus generated (real-looking, heavier).
conventions: Decision recorded as code: a module-level dict with a docstring describing the decision.
Uses a fixed schema-like set of keys: id, meaning, options, scope, subtype, explanation_source, legibility.
Options are objects with label, implication, and an optional recommended boolean.
Provenance is expressed as a code:// URI and annotated in an inline comment.
concerns: The 'recommended' flag is only present on the preferred option, which could make programmatic comparison slightly asymmetric.
The options have no stable ids/keys, so references must rely on labels.
The 'indistinguishable from real' bar is not quantified or validated in this file.
The inline provenance comment mixes human-readable metadata with the code value.
notes: A structured, version-controlled decision ledger entry that recommends a hybrid visual strategy and anchors its rationale to an external gather source.
questions: What criteria decide which visuals count as 'hero showpieces'?
What does 'drawn' mean technically here—vector, procedural, component library, or something else?
How is the 'indistinguishable from real' bar measured or validated?
Who approves or can override this recommended option?
What are the cost, latency, and maintenance implications of generating hero imagery?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches what the file does: it records a visual-fidelity trade-off decision with options, a recommendation, provenance, and explanatory metadata.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to encode a strategic design decision into version-controlled structured data, make the rationale and provenance transparent, and nudge the company toward a hybrid drawn-plus-generated-hero approach.
novelty: It is unusual to store decision records as executable Python dictionaries that include legibility metadata and code-provenance URIs.
contribution: It contributes a structured, auditable decision record for visual fidelity, including a recommended hybrid strategy and a clear provenance link, which a fused system can reference when reconciling design or build plans.
summary_for_embedding: A Python decision ledger entry named DECISION that records the global visual-fidelity trade-off between drawn and generated imagery, lists three options, recommends drawing most visuals and generating only hero showpieces, and cites a gather source for provenance.
intention_for_embedding: This file was created to capture and communicate the company's strategic choice about how photoreal its visuals should be, with a legible rationale and an anchored source.
